attempt.title: Page media devtools bridge caused a homepage render loop
attempt.symptom: Opening the public home page with the localhost-only page-media devtools mounted triggered `Maximum update depth exceeded` from `PageMediaDevtoolsContext.tsx`.
attempt.cause: The provider recreated `setCurrentPage`, `clearPage`, and the full context object on every render. `PageMediaRegistryBridge` depended on that changing context object, so its effect cleanup and re-run kept setting state recursively.
attempt.attempted-fix: Stabilize the context callbacks with `useCallback`, memoize the provider value with `useMemo`, and make the bridge effect depend only on the stable `setCurrentPage` / `clearPage` functions rather than the whole context object.

attempt.title: Portal-access integration test failed before the Clerk id column existed
attempt.symptom: After adding `clerkUserID` to Payload `users`, `tests/int/lib/auth/portalAccess.int.spec.ts` failed with `column users.clerk_user_i_d does not exist` when it created records before the latest schema change had been applied.
attempt.cause: The runtime app/build path had already migrated the database, but the targeted integration test could hit a database that had not seen the new column yet. Calling `payload.db.migrate()` inside the test exposed a second pre-existing issue: older migration files import `MigrateUpArgs` and `MigrateDownArgs` as runtime values, so Payload's dynamic migration loader throws during tests.
attempt.attempted-fix: Keep the new migration file on type-only imports and make the portal-access integration test self-heal by issuing the specific `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` and index creation directly through the Drizzle handle before creating `users` records.

attempt.title: First-time Clerk social sign-in failed because Payload auth user creation still required a password
attempt.symptom: Signing in through Clerk social login from the public shell hit a `ValidationError` on `users.password`, which bubbled into the homepage auth sidebar and returned `GET / 500` during the first request after Google sign-in.
attempt.cause: `resolveCustomerPayloadUser()` correctly mapped existing users by Clerk id or email, but when no Payload user existed yet it created a new document in the auth-enabled `users` collection without a password. Payload auth collections still validate `password` on create even if Clerk is the real identity provider.
attempt.attempted-fix: Assign a random local-only placeholder password when creating a brand-new Clerk-backed Payload user, keep Clerk as the real identity, and add an integration test that covers first-time Clerk customer creation so future auth cleanup does not reintroduce the validation failure.

attempt.title: Organizations collection access helper caused a circular import during Payload boot
attempt.symptom: `payload migrate:create` crashed with `ReferenceError: Cannot access 'canManageOrganizations' before initialization` as soon as the new `organizations` collection imported the access helper.
attempt.cause: The access helper imported collection slug constants directly from the new collections, while the collections imported the access helper. That created a circular module edge during Payload config evaluation.
attempt.attempted-fix: Move shared collection slugs and the default Grime Time Clerk org id into a neutral `src/lib/auth/organizationConstants.ts` module so collections, sync helpers, and access helpers no longer import each other at config-load time.

verification.command: cmd /c npm.cmd run payload -- migrate:create add_organizations_and_memberships --skipEmpty

attempt.title: Membership after-change role sync deadlocked the compatibility bridge
attempt.symptom: The first `organization-memberships` integration tests hung or timed out, and cleanup surfaced a broken `ON DELETE SET NULL`/`NOT NULL` combination when bootstrap-created memberships were left behind after a failed run.
attempt.cause: Trying to recompute and write the legacy `users.roles` bridge inside the membership collection's after-change hook created a fragile same-transaction path. The bridge exists for compatibility, but the real authorization source is now the first-party membership query.
attempt.attempted-fix: Remove the membership after-change role sync hook, keep `organizations`/`organization-memberships` as the real runtime auth source, and move the legacy-role bridge to explicit bootstrap/auth-resolution paths (`organizationSync` and auth entrypoints) where the membership write has already settled.

attempt.title: Clerk-signed Grime Time staff users could sign in but still missed admin tools, impersonation, and page-media controls
attempt.symptom: A real Grime Time staff member signed in through Google/Clerk could land on the homepage but still saw no floating operator toolbar, no impersonation search, no page-media tooling, and `/admin/login` still behaved like a second auth island instead of recognizing the existing session.
attempt.cause: The first-party membership bootstrap only promoted `ADMIN_EMAIL` or `@grimetime.app` users into the Grime Time staff org. Clerk org membership itself was not being read during session resolution, and several internal routes still authorized through legacy `users.roles` rather than the new membership-aware admin check. Payload admin also still required its own cookie with no bridge from Clerk-backed staff identity.
attempt.attempted-fix: Extend the Clerk session identity to include organization memberships, bootstrap the mapped Grime Time staff org from Clerk membership when present, switch impersonation routes to membership-aware admin auth, add a Payload-admin cookie bridge plus `/admin/login` redirect for signed-in Clerk users, and expose direct operator links plus inline homepage media hover controls from the public shell.

attempt.title: Bootstrap membership creation failed at runtime with invalid `organization_id` and `user_id` relationship validation
attempt.symptom: Opening staff-only routes such as `/ops` after Clerk sign-in could throw `ValidationError: The following field is invalid: organization_id, user_id` from `ensureBootstrapOrganizationMembership()`, which then broke auth-context resolution and returned a 500 from the route.
attempt.cause: The new first-party org bootstrap path created or updated `organization-memberships` records through local Payload operations without a local request object. In live runtime resolution, the relationship validation path for `organization` and `user` could not consistently resolve the linked records or access context, even though the same path passed in narrower tests.
attempt.attempted-fix: Create a local Payload request with `createLocalReq({ user }, payload)` inside the bootstrap path, and thread that `req` through organization lookup, organization creation/update, existing membership lookup, and membership create/update so the relationship validation and linked record resolution run under a stable local request context.

attempt.title: Ops shell felt oversized and trapped page scrolling behind inset chrome
attempt.symptom: The staff `/ops` view showed a sidebar that felt too wide, a visual gutter between the sidebar and main content, duplicated workspace actions in the page header, and an inner scroll region that made the page feel like it was scrolling in a strip between the nav and the content. The floating operator toolbar could also overlap the ops header when it snapped to the default top-right corner.
attempt.cause: The portal shell still used the inset sidebar variant plus a broad sidebar width, which created extra margins around the main surface. The ops and customer pages also kept their own nested `overflow-y-auto` containers instead of letting the page scroll naturally, and the toolbar still defaulted to the public-site corner placement even on secured staff routes.
attempt.attempted-fix: Switch the portal shell back to the standard sidebar variant, narrow the shared portal sidebar width, remove the duplicate `Open ops workspace` header action, replace the staff-only `Customer home` nav item with a direct `Public site` route, let the main ops/customer surfaces use normal page flow instead of nested scroll containers, and move the toolbar's default secure-route corner to bottom-right via a versioned storage key.

attempt.title: Ops chart metric controls looked like stray transparent buttons in the header
attempt.symptom: The Business momentum card showed a small metric toggle cluster in the top-right of the header, which read more like overlapping transparent buttons than intentional dashboard controls. The useful metric context strings such as `2 active plans` for MRR were computed in the loader but were not surfaced in a clean place in the UI.
attempt.cause: The chart used a compact ToggleGroup / Select switcher in the header, but the surrounding dashboard had already adopted larger card-like KPI affordances. That left the metric controls visually out of family and disconnected from the real metric context produced in `loadOpsDashboardData()`.
attempt.attempted-fix: Remove the top-right chart toggle cluster, add explicit chart metric summaries from the loader (Pipeline, Revenue, MRR, Margin), and render them as a clickable KPI-style row below the chart so the same controls double as metric context and chart selection. Reuse real detail strings such as `open quotes` and `active plans` instead of hiding them in the loader.

attempt.title: Compact KPI cards still pinned trend pills in the top-right and the ops chart still surfaced a stale “later phase” disclaimer
attempt.symptom: Even after the chart control rewrite, the `/ops` view still showed top-right transparent-looking pills such as `1 active plans` because the compact KPI cards rendered their trend badges as absolutely positioned badges in the card header. The chart disclaimer could also still show the old line about connecting real accounting and CRM data “in a later phase,” especially when that text had already been saved in Internal ops settings.
attempt.cause: The stray element was not from the chart anymore; it was the `SectionCards` compact card layout. Separately, the loader still accepted the legacy disclaimer string from either the global default or previously saved settings.
attempt.attempted-fix: Move KPI trend badges into the normal card flow under the metric value instead of absolutely pinning them in the corner, update the Internal ops settings default disclaimer to describe the real live series, and normalize legacy illustrative disclaimer strings inside the loader so the stale “later phase” copy is replaced even if the old value already exists in the database.

attempt.title: Page-media drawer creation flow was too awkward and the first gallery rewrite left a broken workbench
attempt.symptom: On the home page, the local media drawer still exposed an unfriendly `Create new media record and swap` block instead of a usable gallery/library flow. The first chunked rewrite also left malformed strings, a hidden generate-new-media path, and no clear placeholder card for drag-drop, upload, or generate.
attempt.cause: The original drawer was built around one-off slot forms, not a reusable media library workflow. The first route changes landed the backend capabilities, but the client rewrite was still centered on inline forms and picked up chunked-patch corruption.
attempt.attempted-fix: Rebuild the drawer as a two-tab local media workbench: `Page media` for slot-focused replace/generate/swap actions and `Media library` for record-focused upload, drag-drop, generate, and swap flows. Extend the local devtools route with GET library loading plus `create-only`, `generate-only`, and `swap-existing-reference`, add a placeholder card for new records, and make both page cards and library cards drag-aware with selected-state feedback.

attempt.title: Generate actions opened in the wrong place and the local media tools still treated everything like images
attempt.symptom: Clicking `Generate` in the page-media drawer pushed the prompt down into the old selected-detail block instead of opening inline on the clicked card. The local workbench and hover editor also still showed extra `New record` actions on page media, only accepted image uploads, and could not deliberately create video media from dropped files or generated prompts.
attempt.cause: The page-media workbench still carried forward the old selected-card pattern from the first rewrite, and the route/helper layer only exposed image-generation semantics even though Payload media records themselves already supported video uploads through `mimeType`.
attempt.attempted-fix: Move generate controls inline onto the clicked page/library cards, remove the extra `New record` actions from page-card and hover tooling, add explicit image/video generation toggles, pass `mediaKind` through the local devtools route, return `mimeType` in devtools summaries so previews can render videos, accept image/video uploads everywhere relevant, and add a dedicated OpenAI video helper for the local media flow.

attempt.title: Page-slot generation still mutated shared media records and could not iterate an existing image into video
attempt.symptom: Generating from a page-media slot such as `hero.media` could still target `generate-replace-existing`, which meant one prompt could mutate a shared media record used by multiple page slots. The local media tools also let admins flip a slot from image to video, but the video generation path did not reuse the current image as a reference input.
attempt.cause: The page drawer and inline hover editor still treated generate like record replacement when a slot already had a media id, even though upload-on-page had already shifted to create-and-swap semantics. The OpenAI video helper accepted prompt-only generation but had no path to resolve the current image record into an `input_reference` object.
attempt.attempted-fix: Make page-slot generate always submit `generate-and-swap`, pass the current slot media id as `sourceMediaId`, resolve that media record server-side when the requested output is video, fetch the current image bytes, and send them to the OpenAI video API as an `input_reference.image_url` data URL. Keep library-record generate-in-place available for explicit record iteration, but make page-slot generation always fork a new media record and swap the page relation.

attempt.title: Page-media cards still wasted space on separate replace/generate controls instead of one focused slot editor
attempt.symptom: Inside the page-media drawer, each slot card still carried its own inline action row and detail state. That made the page tab feel crowded, and clicking a layout media card did not open a focused editor for that slot. The upload/generate flows were available, but they were spread across the card instead of living in a single compact slot-edit modal.
attempt.cause: The page tab had evolved incrementally from inline forms. Even after the gallery and media-kind improvements, the slot cards still owned too much UI and treated upload/generate as separate surface actions rather than one “edit this slot” workflow.
attempt.attempted-fix: Condense page-slot actions down to a single sparkle affordance and card click target, then open a focused modal for the selected slot with `Image`, `Video`, and `Gallery` tabs. Move drag-drop/upload to the top of the modal, keep generate controls at the bottom for image/video tabs, add `Cancel` plus a top-right close button, and cover the new modal-first behavior with a component integration test.

attempt.title: Localhost production verification for the page composer and focused copilot flow is now stable
attempt.symptom: The first browser pass could not reach a healthy `next start` surface: Playwright readiness timed out, local production routes returned HTTP 500, and the new page-composer coverage could not execute end to end.
attempt.cause: There were several stacked issues. The page-visibility migration was not idempotent under multi-worker startup, Clerk middleware expected `localhost` rather than `127.0.0.1` during local production rewrites, Node-side ESM resolution was brittle on `next/cache` imports in the Payload boot path, and browser verification exposed additional composer-shell accessibility and overlay-targeting gaps.
attempt.attempted-fix: Make the page-visibility migration idempotent, switch Playwright and login helpers to `localhost`, change the affected `next/cache` imports to `next/cache.js`, then tighten the page-composer drawer and spec together: fix the copilot-authoring render loop, avoid uncontrolled-to-controlled select churn, label the media prompt textarea, label the real close button, and scope browser interactions to the fixed drawer and copilot surfaces so draft-save, media generation, publish, and focused-copilot flows can run reliably.

verification.command: cmd /c npm.cmd exec eslint src/components/admin-impersonation/PageComposerDrawer.tsx tests/e2e/page-composer.e2e.spec.ts playwright.config.ts tests/helpers/login.ts src/migrations/20260402_194706_add_page_visibility.ts
cmd /c npm.cmd exec -- playwright test tests/e2e/page-composer.e2e.spec.ts --config=playwright.config.ts

attempt.title: Block-visibility schema and production-browser reorder verification both needed final hardening before phase 15 could close
attempt.symptom: After phase-15 code added block-level hide/show support, the browser suite failed with missing `is_hidden` columns on page-layout tables. Once the schema was corrected, the production `next start` browser run still exposed a reorder failure because the spec's keyboard drag helper did not consistently drive the DnD handle under the fixed composer drawer.
attempt.cause: The new `isHidden` field had been added to the Payload block schemas and generated types without a matching Postgres migration for the live and version layout-block tables. Separately, the e2e helper was sending keyboard events through `page.keyboard`, which was less reliable than targeting the actual sortable drag handle element inside the drawer.
attempt.attempted-fix: Add a dedicated migration that creates `is_hidden` across every current page-layout block table and version table, migrate the local database before browser verification, then update the Playwright helper to press the sortable handle directly and assert Structure order before saving and publishing.

verification.command: cmd /c npm.cmd exec eslint src/migrations/20260404_174500_add_page_block_visibility.ts src/migrations/index.ts tests/e2e/page-composer.e2e.spec.ts
cmd /c set PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL=http://localhost:3110 && npm.cmd exec -- playwright test --config=playwright.local.config.ts tests/e2e/page-composer.e2e.spec.ts

attempt.title: The first generated attachment migration pulled unrelated schema noise and had to be reduced to the real contract only
attempt.symptom: When the new estimator attachment collection was generated, the migration draft also included unrelated schema churn such as organization-membership enum changes that were not part of the upload contract.
attempt.cause: The local schema snapshot had drifted enough that the generated migration tried to reconcile more than the new `instant_quote_request_attachments` collection.
attempt.attempted-fix: Replace the generated migration body with a clean hand-edited migration that only creates the estimator attachment enums, table, foreign keys, indexes, and locked-document relation needed for `instant-quote-request-attachments`.

attempt.title: Playwright webServer was not reliable for the targeted homepage estimator browser pass, so verification switched to a manual localhost dev server
attempt.symptom: The standard Playwright `webServer` startup path timed out locally, and a manual `next start` on `localhost:3110` still failed because the runtime expected a missing `.next/prerender-manifest.json` even after a successful build.
attempt.cause: The local Next.js startup path remained brittle under the normal Playwright wrapper, and the targeted estimator browser checks only needed a healthy frontend surface plus mocked route responses rather than full production server semantics.
attempt.attempted-fix: Keep build verification as a separate command, then run the focused estimator browser tests against a manually started `next dev --hostname localhost --port 3110` process with a temporary Playwright config that omits `webServer` and points `PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL` at that live server.

verification.command: cmd /c npm.cmd exec -- playwright test tests/e2e/frontend.e2e.spec.ts --grep "instant quote" --config=playwright.local.config.ts

attempt.title: Composer runtime verification required a matching Payload migration and split-surface e2e locator updates
attempt.symptom: After the preview-first composer and reusable/custom-html schema work compiled, the frontend homepage failed at runtime with missing `composer_reusable_*` columns on page-version block tables. Once the migration was applied, the browser spec still failed because old locators treated the drawer as a single-column surface and matched the new preview canvas instead of the active Structure or Media tabs.
attempt.cause: The new block fields and `customHtml` block tables needed a real Postgres migration before the runtime could query page versions. Separately, the e2e spec still assumed the composer only exposed one interaction surface, so button-name locators became ambiguous once the preview canvas and tab panels were both interactive.
attempt.attempted-fix: Generate and apply a dedicated Payload migration for reusable metadata plus `customHtml`, then update the Playwright helpers to scope interactions to the active tabpanel for Structure and Media while leaving preview interaction available separately.

verification.command: cmd /c set PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL=http://localhost:3111 && npm.cmd exec -- playwright test tests/e2e/page-composer.e2e.spec.ts --config=playwright.local.config.ts

attempt.title: Shared-section schema work exposed two Payload persistence mismatches that needed explicit correction before UI work could build safely
attempt.symptom: The first generated `shared-sections` migration created the live enum for `status` as `draft|published` even though the collection contract included `archived`, and the collection hook normalized tags to `string[]` while the Payload field stored tag rows as `{ tag }[]`.
attempt.cause: Payload's generated migration reused the draft-status enum shape for the new `status` column in this collection/version surface, and the conceptual shared-section contract was intentionally flatter than Payload's array-row storage model for tags.
attempt.attempted-fix: Keep the conceptual shared-section contract in helper code, convert tags back to Payload row objects at the collection boundary, and add a follow-up manual migration that inserts `archived` into the relevant shared-section enums after the generated collection migration runs.

verification.command: cmd /c npm.cmd exec eslint src/lib/pages/sharedSections.ts src/lib/auth/sharedSectionPermissions.ts src/collections/SharedSections.ts src/lib/pages/sharedSectionLibrary.ts src/app/api/internal/shared-sections/route.ts tests/int/lib/pages/shared-sections.int.spec.ts tests/int/lib/auth/shared-section-permissions.int.spec.ts tests/int/app/shared-sections-route.int.spec.ts

attempt.title: Local build verification conflicted with stale Next dev and start processes, so the verification pass had to clear the old server state before trusting build artifacts
attempt.symptom: `npm run build` first reported that another Next build was already running because a leftover `next dev --port 3110` process still held `.next/dev/lock`. After that server was stopped, the build command outlived the shell timeout while still writing fresh `.next` production artifacts.
attempt.cause: Phase 14 and 17 browser verification left manual `next dev` and `next start` processes behind for local Playwright passes, and those long-lived processes reused the same `.next` surface that the phase 18 build verification needed.
attempt.attempted-fix: Stop the stale `next dev` process before rerunning build verification, then confirm the build completed by waiting for the active `next build` processes to exit and checking for a fresh `.next/BUILD_ID` plus updated production manifest files.

verification.command: cmd /c npm.cmd exec eslint src/lib/pages/sharedSections.ts src/lib/pages/sharedSectionLibrary.ts src/lib/query/queryKeys.ts src/lib/auth/portalNavSurface.ts src/lib/navigation/portalSidebar.ts src/components/portal/shared-sections/SharedSectionsLibrary.tsx "src/app/(portal)/shared-sections/page.tsx" tests/int/components/portal/shared-sections/shared-sections-library.int.spec.tsx tests/int/lib/navigation/portal-sidebar.int.spec.ts tests/int/lib/auth/portal-nav-surface.int.spec.tsx

attempt.title: Shared-section reusable metadata triggered a Postgres enum-name overflow during Next build
attempt.symptom: `npm run build` failed during Payload init for static-page data collection with `Exceeded max identifier length for table or enum name of 63 characters` on `enum_pages_blocks_testimonials_block_composer_reusable_source_type`.
attempt.cause: The new `composerReusable.sourceType` select field lived under block tables with long generated table names, and Postgres enum identifiers inherited the full nested path. The field contract itself was correct, but the default generated enum name exceeded Postgres's identifier limit.
attempt.attempted-fix: Keep the hidden reusable metadata field, but assign short explicit `enumName` values for the nested `sourceType` and `mode` select fields in `src/blocks/shared/composerReusableField.ts` so Payload's generated Postgres enums stay within identifier limits.
